text: Quantifying the human perception of visual content remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. While numerous mathematical models have been developed to measure image quality, they are often restricted to quantifying technical degradations like noise or blur and remain disconnected from natural language. Furthermore, when evaluating more abstract concepts—the "feel" rather than just the "look" of an image—researchers typically rely on supervised models trained on labor-intensive, human-labeled datasets. In this paper, we move beyond these conventional paradigms by leveraging the rich visual-language priors embedded in Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models. We demonstrate that CLIP can be used to assess both the quality and abstract perception of images without the need for task-specific fine-tuning. By introducing optimized prompt designs and an effective prompt-pairing strategy, we show how to successfully harness these latent priors. Extensive experiments on controlled datasets and standard Image Quality Assessment (IQA) benchmarks reveal that CLIP captures sophisticated perceptual nuances that generalize across a wide range of aesthetic and technical evaluations.
